summary: The post examines why software developers struggle to monetize their own code despite their skills being in high demand. It traces three historical phases of developer income: selling code (1980s-90s), selling time/skills (late 1990s-present), and an emerging model of selling access based on reputation. Code is hard to monetize because it has zero marginal cost and developers can easily build alternatives to existing tools. Employment solves the income problem but forces developers to write code for others rather than themselves. The post argues that the future of developer economics lies in monetizing reputation and identity rather than code or time.
key_points: Developers' code-writing skills have market value, but the code they independently produce often has none, creating a fundamental tension
Code is difficult to monetize for two reasons: zero marginal cost enabling free sharing, and developers' ability and desire to build their own alternatives
Selling time through employment or freelancing is a workaround that funds developers' lifestyles but doesn't let them profit from their own public code
As automation advances, the amount of code that needs to be written by humans is shrinking, threatening the 'sell time' model long-term
Reputation-based income — charging for prioritization, knowledge, or brand — may be the emerging model because a developer's reputation has fewer substitutes than their code
money_quotes: While their code-writing skills have market value, their output (the code they write) has none.
Selling to developers a bit like selling ice to an Eskimo.
Information wants to be freely shared, despite our best efforts to make it behave otherwise.
Over time, we don't need to write as much code as we used to.
Selling time doesn't allow a developer to make money producing their own code. It merely funds their lifestyle so they can write other code in their spare time.
Maybe it's not the projects, but the people behind the projects, that carry the most value.

So long as we focus on code, we'll keep coming back to the problem of substitutes. Code has many substitutes. Getting paid to write code has many substitutes. But a developer's reputation has fewer substitutes.
In the early 1990s, it seemed inconceivable that people wouldn't pay for software. Today, it might seem inconceivable that developers might eventually not sell their time.
key_insight: The fundamental challenge for developers is that code and coding skills are endlessly substitutable, so lasting value lies not in what developers produce but in the irreplaceable reputations they build.
